title: JSONSchemaBench: A Rigorous Benchmark of Structured Outputs for Language Models
authors: Saibo Geng (EPFL), Hudson Cooper (Microsoft), Michał Moskal (Microsoft), Samuel Jenkins (Microsoft), Julian Berman (JSON Schema), Nathan Ranchin (EPFL), Robert West (EPFL / Microsoft), Eric Horvitz (Microsoft), Harsha Nori (Microsoft)
venue: arXiv preprint, cs.CL (cross-listed cs.AI); arXiv:2501.10868, v3 (27 Feb 2025). Not noted as peer-reviewed as of fetch date.
claim: Little systematic work has evaluated constrained decoding for structured (JSON Schema) outputs, so the paper introduces JSONSchemaBench, a 10K-schema real-world benchmark, and a three-axis evaluation framework (efficiency, coverage, quality) applied to six constrained-decoding frameworks (Guidance, Outlines, Llamacpp, XGrammar, OpenAI, Gemini). Its headline findings are that constrained decoding can speed generation by up to 50%, frameworks vary widely in real-world schema coverage (best supports ~2x as many schemas as worst), and constrained decoding improves downstream task accuracy by up to ~4% even on minimally-structured tasks like GSM8K.
novelty: The authors present three contributions versus prior work: (1) assembling and organizing ~10K real-world JSON schemas (from GitHub, JSON Schema Store, GlaiveAI function-calling data, Kubernetes configs, Snowplow, Washington Post ANS) into a graded benchmark (JSONSchemaBench) that is larger and more realistic than prior formal-grammar-based or narrowly task-specific constrained-decoding evaluations; (2) a fine-grained multi-notion coverage framework distinguishing Declared, Empirical, True, and Theoretical coverage plus a derived Compliance Rate metric, which prior work lacked; (3) a first cross-framework evaluation of six state-of-the-art constrained-decoding engines along efficiency, coverage, and quality simultaneously, whereas prior studies (Roy et al. 2024; Tang et al. 2024; Yao et al. 2023a) either compared constrained vs. unconstrained decoding without cross-framework comparison or used narrow/artificial benchmarks (Sec. 2 Background and Related Work).

protocol.description: The paper's core evaluations are single forward-pass generations, not interactive/multi-turn protocols. For the Coverage experiment (Sec. 5.1 Setup): 'The prompt consists of a simple instruction with two-shot examples (Figure 3), and validation is performed using the jsonschema Python library... We use greedy decoding with zero-temperature, performing a single generation run.' Appendix B confirms: 'We use greedy decoding with no top P or top K sampling for all the experiments. We only get one output from the model, which we will use to validate the schema compliance... The fact that we only sample the top 1 output may quantify our empirical coverage as Top 1 Empirical Coverage.' For the Quality experiment (Sec. 6.1-6.2), models see a CoT-eliciting prompt and must produce a single JSON object {"reasoning": ..., "answer": ...} in one generation (Table 7); this is also single-shot per instance (no iterative refinement or multi-turn feedback loop described anywhere in the paper).
protocol.observation_content: For Coverage: model sees a schema plus a 'simple instruction with two-shot examples' per the described prompt template (Figure 3, not text-renderable — flagged [FIGURE] in extraction, so the exact prompt text is unverified — missing from extraction beyond the caption 'Prompt template used to generate JSON objects in the coverage experiment'). For Quality: model sees the task input (e.g., 'Ian Peter Bernard Stephen' for Last Letter, a shuffle-objects scenario, or a GSM8K word problem) and must produce CoT reasoning plus a final answer, structured as {"reasoning": <reasoning>, "answer": <final answer>} (Table 7, Sec. 6.1); a full instantiated example prompt is only given via Figure 7 ('Prompt template for solving GSM8K with JSON responses'), which is a caption-only figure not rendered as text (Appendix F) — exact full prompt text is unverified — missing from extraction.

representations.summary: The paper does not run a controlled ablation over input/output representation format for a single task the way c11 would (e.g., comparing key-orderings or quoting conventions). Its closest representation-relevant finding is about decoding constraints changing token-level representation and downstream accuracy: constrained JSON output (forcing e.g. integer-only answers, blocking commas in numbers) changed task accuracy versus unconstrained free-text generation. Sec. 6 gives a worked example: 'an LM might answer '89,000' instead of the correct '89000' in a GSM8K question. Constrained decoding can block the invalid token ',', enforcing structural compliance but potentially may cause the LM to go out of distribution and generate '890000' instead.' Across all three quality tasks, every constrained framework outperformed the unconstrained ('LM only') baseline (Table 8, Sec. 6.2), suggesting that forcing a canonical/simplified output representation (JSON with fixed schema, e.g. answer as bare integer or single letter) helped rather than hurt accuracy in this setup, contrary to the 'format restrictions hurt performance' claim from Tam et al. 2024 that motivated the study.

models.name: Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct
models.key_results: Used for the Coverage experiment only (Sec. 5.1). Empirical coverage varies by dataset/engine (Table 4, not fully machine-extracted in this local copy — flagged as a table gap; only prose summary available): 'Guidance shows the highest empirical coverage on six out of the eight datasets, with Llamacpp taking the lead on the remaining two... LM-only approaches achieve acceptable coverage on easy-to-medium datasets but show significant performance drops on harder datasets' (Sec. 5.2).

models.name: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
strategies: CoT reasoning + structured JSON answer field
constrained decoding via 6 frameworks (Guidance, Outlines, Llamacpp, XGrammar, OpenAI, Gemini) vs. LM-only
models.key_results: Used for Efficiency (Sec. 4.1, quantized Q8bit for Appendix E) and Quality (Sec. 6.1-6.2) experiments. Quality results, Table 8: LM only 50.7%/52.6%/80.1% (Last Letters/Shuffle Objects/GSM8K); XGrammar 51.2%/52.7%/83.7%; Llamacpp 52.0%/52.6%/82.4%; Outlines 53.3%/53.0%/81.6%; Guidance 54.0%/55.9%/83.8%. 'Guidance consistently delivers the best performance across all tasks, with approximately a 3% improvement over the LM-only approach in every task' (Sec. 6.2).

models.name: OpenAI / Gemini (proprietary, closed-source structured-output APIs)
models.key_results: 'closed-source grammar engines consistently have the lowest coverage; they came in last on all but one dataset' but 'While closed-source implementations have low empirical coverage, they have very high compliance rates, indicating that their providers have taken a more conservative strategy' (Sec. 5.2, Compliance Rate paragraph). Empirical coverage not directly comparable to open-source engines because underlying models differ (Table 4 caption).

headline_numbers: Constrained decoding speeds generation by up to 50% vs. unconstrained decoding (Sec. 1 Introduction, finding (1)).

Best constrained-decoding framework supports roughly 2x as many real-world schemas (empirical coverage) as the worst framework (Sec. 1 Introduction, finding (2); detailed per-dataset breakdown in Table 4, prose in Sec. 5.2).

Constrained decoding improves downstream task accuracy by up to ~4% even on minimally-structured tasks like GSM8K (Sec. 1 Introduction, finding (3)); concretely Guidance gives ~3% improvement over LM-only on every one of the three quality tasks (Table 8, Sec. 6.2): Last Letters 50.7%->54.0%, Shuffle Objects 52.6%->55.9%, GSM8K 80.1%->83.8%.

Guidance achieves full JSON Schema Test Suite coverage on 13 of 43 categories and moderate coverage (>50%) on 21; Llamacpp/XGrammar reach full coverage on only 1 category each; Outlines reaches no full-coverage category (Table 5, Sec. 5.3.1).

Benchmark scale: 9,558 total schemas across 10 named datasets after filtering (Table 1, Sec. 3.1), drawn from a superset of ~10K schemas referenced in the abstract/intro; sizes/complexity vary hugely, e.g. Kubernetes schemas up to 818.6 KB / 11,720 fields, JSONSchemaStore up to 2,934.8 KB / 108,292 fields (Table 9, Appendix A).
failure_modes: Two formally defined failure modes for grammar engines against the JSON Schema Test Suite (Sec. 5.3, Definitions 5.4-5.5): Over-constrained (engine rejects JSON instances that are actually valid under the schema -- too strict) and Under-constrained (engine allows JSON instances that are actually invalid -- too permissive). Table 6 (not machine-extracted as cell data in this local copy, prose only) reports: 'Outlines, Llamacpp, and Guidance follow a consistent failure pattern, with most errors occurring during compilation and over-constrained failures being more frequent than under-constrained ones. In contrast, XGrammar minimizes compilation errors but shows the highest number of under-constrained failures, indicating a trade-off favoring permissiveness' (Sec. 5.3.1 Failure Analysis). Outlines specifically times out (40s-10min) on schemas using minItems/maxItems/enum/Array features, driving its low Compliance Rate (Sec. 5.2). LM-only (no constrained decoding) has the lowest compliance rate overall, 'highlighting its unreliability as a standalone solution' (Sec. 5.2). A quality-specific failure mode is hypothesized (not directly measured as a coverage failure but as an accuracy-degrading mechanism): constrained decoding can force the model off-distribution by blocking otherwise-natural tokens (e.g., blocking the comma in '89,000' could push the model to emit '890000' instead of '89000') (Sec. 6, hypothetical example).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper only evaluates single forward-pass / single-sample generation throughout (Top-1 Empirical Coverage, Appendix B: 'We only get one output from the model... The fact that we only sample the top 1 output may quantify our empirical coverage as Top 1 Empirical Coverage'). It explicitly notes this as a limitation/design choice, not something it studied comparatively: 'It's totally plausible to sample more outputs and validate them all, and it might detect more schema violations' (Appendix B). There is no interactive, multi-turn, or iterative-refinement condition anywhere in the paper -- no experiment gives the model feedback and a chance to retry. All reported gains (efficiency speedup, coverage differences, quality improvements) are attributed to constrained decoding operating within a single generation pass, not to any multi-step/interactive protocol.

relation_to_candidate.position: This paper sits far from c11 on the key protocol axes. It is single-shot, single-sample generation (Top-1, greedy, no retries, no multi-turn feedback) as established above. What is observed by the model is a JSON Schema definition (a structural/type constraint specification, e.g. 'this field must be an integer between 0 and 100') plus a short instruction/few-shot prompt -- not a full worked example of an instance and its unique canonical serialization under invented deviation rules. The output shape is a full JSON *instance* that must validate against the schema (a complete structured object, e.g. a function-call payload or Kubernetes config fragment) -- this is closer to c11's 'emit a full structured output' shape than to a single-fact/label task, but the correctness criterion is schema-conformance (many valid strings pass) rather than c11's whole-string exact match against one unique canonical reference string. The verifier type is a schema validator (jsonschema Python library, Draft 2020-12, with format checks) that accepts a large equivalence class of correct outputs (Sec. 5.1, Appendix B) -- structurally the opposite of c11's zero-partial-credit single-reference-string exact-match verifier.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both are structured-JSON-output evaluations concerned with real-world schema diversity and complexity grading (JSONSchemaBench's trivial/easy/medium/hard/ultra splits by field count, Table 1/Appendix A, mirror c11's need for a graded real-world schema pool). Both use large, non-trivial, non-toy schema collections drawn from public repositories. Both care about a bespoke/deviant convention layer on top of a JSON substrate to some degree -- though JSONSchemaBench's 'convention' is the standard JSON Schema spec itself (Draft 2020-12), not an invented house style.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Task type: JSONSchemaBench asks for *any schema-valid instance*, an underconstrained generative task scored by a validity checker; c11 asks for *the one canonical serialization* of a given input under invented deviation rules, scored by exact string match -- a fully-constrained, deterministic-target task. (2) Standard vs. invented conventions: JSONSchemaBench evaluates conformance to the public, well-documented JSON Schema standard; c11 deliberately requires deviating from public standards (RFC 8785 JCS) via invented house rules, which cannot be looked up or partially inferred from training-data familiarity with the real standard. (3) Verifier granularity: schema validation is class-based (accepts a broad equivalence class of correct JSON); c11's scoring is single-string exact match with zero partial credit. (4) Protocol subject: JSONSchemaBench studies constrained-decoding *frameworks/engines* (Guidance, Outlines, XGrammar, etc.) as the primary object of comparison, with the LM held mostly fixed per experiment; c11 studies the LM's own latent-rule-following/reconstruction ability directly, with no engine-level grammar constraint imposed by an external tool. (5) No contamination-proofing-by-regeneration mechanism is described in this paper (see corpus_claim_verdict).
relation_to_candidate.what_c11_would_add: c11 would add: (a) a deterministic, single-reference-answer target (exact-match against a ~40-line reference canonicalizer) versus JSONSchemaBench's broad schema-validity acceptance criterion; (b) explicit adversarial deviation from public standards so the task cannot be solved by recalling/matching a known spec, unlike JSONSchemaBench's use of the actual public JSON Schema standard; (c) per-rule stratified diagnosis (each violated house rule visible in the diff) versus JSONSchemaBench's engine-level pass/fail coverage counts; (d) direct measurement of an LM's rule-following ability unmediated by an external constrained-decoding engine, whereas JSONSchemaBench's central subject is the engines themselves.

corpus_claim: JSONSchemaBench (Geng et al., 2025) is c11's schema-pool donor: a graded real-world schema corpus for calibrating canonicalization difficulty, contamination-proofed by regeneration.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported, with a correction. The 'graded real-world schema corpus' part is well-supported: JSONSchemaBench is explicitly organized into complexity tiers (trivial/small(easy)/medium/large(hard)/ultra by field count, Table 1 and Appendix A: 'we split the data into five collections based on the schema size: trivial, small, medium, large, ultra') drawn from real-world sources (GitHub, GlaiveAI, Kubernetes, Snowplow, Washington Post, JSON Schema Store). However: (1) the corpus grades *schema structural complexity* (for constrained-decoding coverage/compliance testing), not 'canonicalization difficulty' in c11's sense (invented serialization/formatting house rules) -- the paper never discusses canonical-string serialization, key ordering, quoting, or any RFC-8785-adjacent concept at all; this is an extrapolation, not something the paper states. (2) 'Contamination-proofed by regeneration' is unsupported/miscited: the paper describes a static, one-time collection-and-filtering process (Sec. 3.1, Appendix A.1: validation, deduplication, cleaning of a fixed 9,558-schema set) with no mechanism for per-run or per-evaluation regeneration of schemas, and the words 'contamination' and 'regenerat*' do not appear anywhere in the paper. If anything, the released static schema set (public GitHub repo) is a contamination *risk* for future model training, not a contamination-proofing mechanism -- the paper does not address this concern at all. The claim as written should be corrected to: JSONSchemaBench is a graded real-world *JSON Schema* (not canonicalization) corpus, useful only as a possible seed/donor pool of realistic schema *shapes* for c11 to build instances from; it provides no contamination-proofing-by-regeneration mechanism, and c11 would need to add its own regeneration/synthesis step (e.g., via json-schema-faker, per c11.md) to get that property.
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dials: schema complexity tiers are field-count-based (trivial <10 fields, easy 10-30, medium 30-100, hard 100-500, ultra >500; Appendix A Sources list) plus measured stats per dataset in Table 9 -- median/max size in KB, field count, max fan-out, and schema depth (e.g. GlaiveAI-2K: 21/44 fields, depth 5/8; JSONSchemaStore: 155/108292 fields, depth 6/22). These give concrete, citable difficulty axes (size, field count, fan-out, nesting depth) that a canonicalization-difficulty dial could borrow if adapting real schemas as a seed pool.

Prompt format effects: the coverage experiment used 'a simple instruction with two-shot examples' (Sec. 5.1) -- exact prompt text not recovered in this extraction (Figure 3 is caption-only); no controlled ablation of prompt format vs. accuracy is reported in the paper (i.e., this paper does not itself provide format-vs-accuracy swing data usable for baseline design beyond the single format tested).

Metrics used: Declared Coverage (framework doesn't crash/reject), Empirical Coverage (output validates against schema), True Coverage (theoretical ideal, unmeasurable), Theoretical Coverage (spec-feature-support based on documentation, Appendix C), and derived Compliance Rate = Empirical/Declared (Sec. 5, Definitions 5.1-5.3 and Appendix C Definition C.1). These four-tier coverage/compliance framework could inform how c11 defines partial-credit-free but multi-faceted diagnostic metrics (e.g., per-rule pass rate as an 'empirical coverage' analog), though c11 itself uses whole-string exact match as sole score.

Floors/ceilings observed: Theoretical coverage (Table 12) ranges from 0.00 (LM-only, by definition) up to 0.96 (Guidance on GlaiveAI) down to 0.13-0.31 for the hardest dataset (JsonSchemaStore: Guidance 0.31 down to XGrammar 0.13, OpenAI 0.00) and 0.33-0.54 for GitHub Hard (Guidance 0.54 down to XGrammar 0.33, OpenAI 0.00) across engines -- illustrating that even well-engineered constrained-decoding frameworks have real ceilings well below 100% on hard real-world schemas, a useful analog for expecting c11's own ceiling to degrade on harder/rule-dense instances. Quality-task accuracy ceiling in this paper tops out around 83.8% (GSM8K, Guidance, Table 8) -- ~16% of even the 'easiest' numeric-answer task fails despite constrained decoding, useful context for calibrating expectations of small-model performance on structured-output correctness tasks generally.

Contamination/data-release status: JSONSchemaBench is fully publicly released at https://github.com/guidance-ai/jsonschemabench (Abstract, Sec. 7 Conclusion) as a static, versioned collection -- there is no regeneration or contamination-proofing mechanism; a public, static, real-world-sourced schema corpus of this kind is itself a contamination risk for any LLM trained after its release, which is the opposite of what the corpus_claim asserts and is directly relevant baseline-design context: c11 should not assume donor schema pools from public benchmarks are contamination-safe without its own regeneration/synthesis layer.
quotes: "We introduce JSONSchemaBench, a benchmark for constrained decoding comprising 10K real-world JSON schemas that encompass a wide range of constraints with varying complexity." (Abstract)
"For more fine-grained evaluation, we split the data into five collections based on the schema size: trivial, small, medium, large, ultra." (Sec. 3.1 Data Collection)

"The prompt consists of a simple instruction with two-shot examples (Figure 3), and validation is performed using the jsonschema Python library... We use greedy decoding with zero-temperature, performing a single generation run." (Sec. 5.1 Setup)

"We only get one output from the model, which we will use to validate the schema compliance... The fact that we only sample the top 1 output may quantify our empirical coverage as Top 1 Empirical Coverage." (Appendix B, Decoding Method)

"Guidance consistently delivers the best performance across all tasks, with approximately a 3% improvement over the LM-only approach in every task. We believe this may be attributed to its token-healing implementation." (Sec. 6.2 Results)

"A framework is over-constrained if it rejects JSON instances that are valid according to a given JSON Schema... A framework is under-constrained if it allows JSON instances that are invalid according to a given JSON Schema." (Sec. 5.3, Definitions 5.4-5.5)

"(1) Constrained decoding can speed up the generation process by 50% compared to unconstrained decoding. (2) Frameworks demonstrate significant differences in their actual support for real-world JSON schemas, with the best framework supporting twice as many schemas as the worst. (3) Constrained decoding consistently improves the performance of downstream tasks up to 4%, even for tasks with minimal structure like GSM8k." (Sec. 1 Introduction)

corrections: novelty field misattributed the Sec. 2 related-work citation as 'Tam et al. 2024' when the source text reads '[Roy et al., 2024; Tang et al., 2024; Yao et al., 2023a]' -- Tang et al. 2024 is the Struc-Bench paper (Xiangru Tang et al.), a distinct citation from Tam et al. 2024 ('Let me speak freely?', Zhi Rui Tam et al.), which is used correctly elsewhere in the dossier (representations, corpus context). Fixed to 'Tang et al. 2024'.

The Sec. 1 Introduction verbatim quote in the quotes array truncated the source sentence '...up to 4%, even for tasks with minimal structure like GSM8k.' to '...up to 4%.' without an ellipsis, misrepresenting a partial quote as complete. Restored the full sentence including the GSM8k clause.

baseline_relevant's 'Floors/ceilings observed' entry compressed Table 12's ranges as a single '0.13-0.31' band covering both JsonSchemaStore and GitHub Hard, but GitHub Hard's actual non-zero engine range in Table 12 is 0.33-0.54 (Guidance 0.54 down to XGrammar 0.33), not 0.13-0.31 (which applies only to JsonSchemaStore). Split into two correctly-attributed ranges.
